evals.prompt: Write an RFC for adding webhook support. Users should be able to register URLs and get notified when certain events happen (order completed, user signed up, payment failed). Think Stripe-style webhooks.
evals.expected_output: An RFC that describes the current notification landscape, neutrally explains why webhooks are needed, and proposes a readable design.

assertions.name: describes-current-state
assertions.description: The RFC has a clear section or paragraph explaining how things work TODAY — what notification mechanisms exist (if any), how users currently learn about events. Not just 'we don't have webhooks' but a real description of the status quo.

assertions.name: current-state-drawbacks-brief
assertions.description: The RFC briefly and factually explains what's insufficient about the current state. No dramatic language, no manufactured urgency — just 'here's what doesn't work and why'. Should be 2-4 sentences, not a rant.

assertions.name: neutral-tone
assertions.description: The RFC reads as neutral analysis, not advocacy. No superlatives, hype, value claims, or unqualified assertions like 'industry standard'. It presents facts and lets the reader decide.

assertions.name: honest-drawbacks-of-proposal
assertions.description: The Drawbacks section acknowledges real costs of the PROPOSED solution. Complexity, maintenance burden, failure modes, operational overhead. Not perfunctory.

assertions.name: alternatives-not-strawmen
assertions.description: The Alternatives Considered section has at least 2 genuine alternatives with honest 'why rejected' reasoning. The rejected options should sound like things a reasonable engineer might actually propose.

assertions.name: readable-not-code-dump
assertions.description: Reads like a design conversation, not a code report. Describes systems by what they do (capabilities, behaviors) not what they're called (module names, file paths). Under 30 inline backtick references is good.

assertions.name: includes-diagrams
assertions.description: Includes at least 1 Mermaid diagram that clarifies a flow better than prose would.

assertions.name: template-structure
assertions.description: Follows the RFC template with Summary, Motivation, Detailed Design, and Rollout Plan sections at minimum.

evals.prompt: Write an RFC for migrating the user import flow from the legacy API to the new backend. Currently the CSV upload goes through the old Node.js API and we need to rebuild it as a new REST endpoint in the primary backend.
evals.expected_output: An RFC that describes the current import flow at the design level, explains neutrally why migration is needed, and proposes a readable implementation plan.

assertions.name: describes-current-state
assertions.description: The RFC explains how the import currently works — the flow, key steps, what happens to the file. Demonstrates understanding without listing every module and file path.

assertions.name: current-state-drawbacks-brief
assertions.description: Explains briefly why the current implementation needs to move. Factual, not dramatic. 2-4 sentences.

assertions.name: neutral-tone
assertions.description: Doesn't trash the legacy system or oversell the new one. Treats the migration as an engineering task, not a crusade.

assertions.name: honest-drawbacks-of-proposal
assertions.description: Acknowledges real costs of the migration — dual maintenance during transition, risk of behavioral differences, testing burden.

assertions.name: rollout-addresses-transition
assertions.description: The rollout plan addresses how both systems coexist, how traffic is shifted, and includes a rollback strategy.

assertions.name: readable-not-code-dump
assertions.description: Reads like a design conversation, not a code report. Describes systems by what they do (capabilities, behaviors) not what they're called (module names, file paths). Under 30 inline backtick references is good.

assertions.name: includes-diagrams
assertions.description: Includes at least 1 Mermaid diagram that clarifies a flow better than prose would.

assertions.name: template-structure
assertions.description: Follows the RFC template with Summary, Motivation, Detailed Design, and Rollout Plan at minimum.

evals.prompt: Write an RFC for adding rate limiting to the API. Some customers are doing thousands of requests per minute and it's affecting other tenants.
evals.expected_output: An RFC that describes the current rate limiting state at the design level, proposes a design without overselling, and honestly weighs the tradeoffs.

assertions.name: describes-current-state
assertions.description: Describes what rate limiting or throttling exists today — the mechanism, the limits, the gaps.

assertions.name: current-state-drawbacks-brief
assertions.description: Briefly and factually explains why the current state is a problem — noisy neighbor effect, no per-tenant fairness. No catastrophizing.

assertions.name: neutral-tone
assertions.description: Presents rate limiting as a standard infrastructure concern. Doesn't blame customers for high usage. Factual throughout.

assertions.name: honest-drawbacks-of-proposal
assertions.description: Acknowledges real costs: added latency per request, operational complexity, risk of false positives blocking legitimate traffic, customer communication needed.

assertions.name: alternatives-substantive
assertions.description: Compares at least 2 real rate limiting strategies with genuine tradeoffs.

assertions.name: readable-not-code-dump
assertions.description: Reads like a design conversation, not a code report. Describes systems by what they do (capabilities, behaviors) not what they're called (module names, file paths). Under 30 inline backtick references is good.

assertions.name: includes-diagrams
assertions.description: Includes at least 1 Mermaid diagram that clarifies a flow better than prose would.

assertions.name: template-structure
assertions.description: Follows the RFC template with Summary, Motivation, Detailed Design, and Rollout Plan at minimum.
